title: The Quest for Neuroplasticity
excerpt: The same restlessness that pulls my attention in a lecture is the same force that pushes me to question everything and learn more.
body:
It has been a volatile month for portfolios. My personal account, now roughly a year old, has taken a beating. The microcap screener's top 10 list, on the other hand, has held up well through the Iran conflict, staying balanced and moderately profitable in a period of real uncertainty. That alone has been encouraging to watch. But I want to take this entry in a different direction. I want to talk about the brain.

I think the same impulse that drives me to learn as much as I can outside the classroom is the same one that pushes me to try every avenue possible and consume information from every direction. My brain does not like to shut down. I think all day, every day. I take in information in class, then teach myself more at home, and as a college student I am admittedly still partially hooked on social media. A brain that never turns off can be a liability, but it is also a strength. The same restlessness that causes me to lose focus daydreaming in a lecture or roll around in bed when I cannot sleep is the same force that pushes me to question everything around me and learn more. I cannot shake the feeling that I am meant to build something. Whether that is a company or a firm I do not know, but it will happen. There is something to be said about a naive confidence that you will succeed. I have no problem failing and trying again because I know that eventually I will find the thing that sticks.

As I mentioned in my last post, this can also be a hindrance. If you keep jumping from interest to interest you never develop real depth. You risk being perpetually distracted by shiny things, letting novelty pull your attention away from the main objective. But there is an advantage to that restlessness too, and I want to explain why.

I have yet to read his book, but James Clear sends out a Thursday email thread called 3-2-1: three ideas, two quotes, and one question. His question this week was, *"What's holding you back right now?"* I read it with a clear mind in the sauna and sat with it for a while. If you walk to the top of a building in Chicago and look out over miles of infrastructure, you are looking at millions of people. What makes you different can be something as simple as telling yourself *right now*. Compared to many people already working in finance I know very little about investing. I have my feet under me, I am not oblivious, but despite being green I made Allegory anyway. I am stealing this framing from James, but I implore you to do that thing right now. There is no reason not to. Time will pass no matter what you do. You might embarrass yourself. Only my girlfriend and I read these posts at the moment. You might fall on your face. You might have no clue what you are doing. But you have the want, and that is enough to start.

I have been given the opportunity to talk to managing directors, analysts, and people at every level of various investment firms. Every single one of them gets excited to share their experience when I tell them I am interested in investing and that I am learning as much as I can while being fully aware that I know next to nothing. Come at life with an open mind and a willingness to learn while understanding that you do not have all the answers, and you will get to where you want to be.

I pride myself in not being conventional. If there were any other direct path into finance I would not be in college. I would be out of my parents' house building my career instead of sitting through subjects that do not interest me just to earn a certificate that tells people I can follow directions. That is not a knock on college itself, it is an acknowledgment that different types of people perform best through different pipelines. I talked to my father about this. Some people are meant to be great CIOs and some are meant to be great analysts. That does not mean one carries more human value than the other, but they do not thrive going through the same system. This is what draws me to people like Elon Musk and Palmer Luckey.

They come from different backgrounds but share a genuine intellectual curiosity and a neuroplasticity that I find compelling. Musk went through traditional schooling but was checked out during class. As a habitual self-teacher he was drawn to what I dub the "quest for knowledge," devouring encyclopedias and textbooks on his own time, learning by obsession rather than obligation. Luckey's path was different entirely. His mother pulled him out of school at an early age, giving him the ability to learn in depth within a customized environment that fostered his curiosity rather than tried to box it in. No windowless fluorescent-lit classrooms, no being forced to learn at a slower pace and demonstrate memorization rather than fundamental understanding. Two different upbringings united under a direct quest for knowledge and an autodidactic style of learning.

My biggest frustration in finding people like this on a college campus is that they are doing exactly what I am; going to classes, doing the work quickly enough to get by with adequate grades, then running back to their room to work on what actually matters to them. I have no doubt there are people at every school who learn this way, but they, like me, are too busy learning to find each other.

I want to bring this back to the CIO versus analyst distinction because I think it illustrates the point. I am not especially detail-oriented in an obsessive, granular way. I know I will be a sufficient analyst when the time comes for me to fill that role, but my real strength lies elsewhere. I understand the world in systems, I always have. For me to fully grasp a framework I need to trace every input, reaction, interaction, and output. It is not enough to know that a DCF starts with EBIT, then you adjust for tax, depreciation and amortization, capital expenditures, and changes in working capital. I will get frustrated if I do not understand the *why* behind every piece and how they all interact. I can stare off into space and mentally model a DCF, see where everything connects and how one cell affects another. That is where my brain thrives. But it means I cannot just memorize and regurgitate information in the way that school grades and GPAs reward, which brings me back to the broader point.

This post is a testament to retaining neuroplasticity and committing to an autodidactic style of learning. A fund manager recently told me that my generation does not read enough books. I take that as another way of saying we have become so averse to challenge that we would rather sit and scroll for dopamine, knowing full well it is killing off our brain cells and even nicknaming the content "brainrot," than exist in the world and train our minds to grow. We live in a time where working out has become mainstream and physical health is a top priority for a majority of people. Why we do not take the same approach with our brains is beyond me. If you can train your brain and retain neuroplasticity in a way where you are always learning and building understanding, there is no ceiling on your potential.

I want to tie this together with the relationship between money and fulfillment. Research on salary and happiness has shown that income improves well-being up to a point, but the slope flattens as you climb. What those studies often leave out is context. Making a hundred thousand dollars a year in rural America with low taxes and a low cost of living is a fundamentally different life than making the same salary in New York City. The threshold where money stops buying happiness is less about the number and more about whether you can live comfortably below your means. Once the financial stress is gone, what actually drives fulfillment is how you spend your time. If you are living below your means and free to learn autodidactically, you are in a position to innovate into something that money alone could never buy you. And even if that innovation never materializes into a company or a career breakthrough, you have already accomplished something most people never will. You have found joy in learning itself. Not learning as school defines it. Real learning, the kind driven by intellectual curiosity that cannot be graded or measured by a GPA.

Train your brain. Play into your strengths. Invest your time in what makes you intellectually curious. In a world addicted to short-term dopamine, that is the edge no one is talking about.

title: Staying on Task
excerpt: There is always a shiny new toy to catch my eye. Allegory helps me stay focused on the task at hand.
body:
Staying on task has been the hardest thing to do while learning about investing. There is always a shiny new toy to catch my eye, whether it be a new spin-off, merger, news story, or global economic event. I know I cannot learn everything all at once, but I keep trying to — that is part of the reason I made this blog. I want to set clear goals and hold myself accountable. If I know people expect to see results from me, I am sure it will help me stay on task. I almost got distracted today with oil prices and the possibility of the Strait of Hormuz being cut off. If I had more extensive knowledge and decades worth of experience, I may have taken action, but Allegory proved useful. I have a task at hand already. If I go off on a tangent I might learn something, but I cannot devote a whole week to it. I know I am not going to act on the news and I would be too late anyway. I will of course continue to keep up with current events and observe how the market reacts, but I already have a task at hand.

The new microcap list will come out tomorrow as March begins. I remind you, those that stay on the list will keep their percent change from the previous month. As far as the full analyst briefs go, I am still working on the automated system. There have been a few hiccups, so I am working on how to mesh my AI tools with Python scripts that pull data from the SEC's website. The biggest problem I have faced with the AI tools is that they need a hard-stop factual baseline. When pulling from SEC filings, they can mix up different data points if the API defines figures with different names — for instance earnings can be named, "Last Twelve Months Earnings From Sector X" as opposed to "TTM.", or anything in between. Each filer has a different classification that can confuse the system. Right now I am working to automate a hard-stop reference point. Once I can pin that down, I believe I will be able to provide error-free coverages to you.

As for my role in the project, to continue building my fundamental skills I will be picking a company from the new top 10 ranking to do a deep dive on for you all. I will forecast the company and do my own writeup separate from the AI and Python writeups I am working on. This will be published when done or by the end of the month. I do not know how long it will take, as the last writeup I did was mostly a learning process so it took longer. I look forward to continuing to document my progress and interacting as this site grows.

title: Welcome to Alleogry
excerpt: Allegory will document my journey from a bootstrapped dorm room operation to a fully functioning hedge fund.
body:
This is my first full entry here on Allegory, and while I expect the writing to sharpen over time, you have to start somewhere. So here it is.
Allegory will document my journey from a bootstrapped dorm room operation to a fully functioning hedge fund. I currently have roughly $2,100 in my brokerage account. Between my part-time internship and the future revenues of this site, I intend to grow that number and bring you along for the ride.

**The Problem**
I am currently building a set of tools that I will be releasing here shortly. The first one was born out of frustration.
I would set screening parameters on ThinkorSwim, and thousands of tickers would flood the screen  prices, volumes, percent changes, the works. The first time I decided to take a deep dive on one of those names, I got lucky. After hours of reading through tickers, pulling up company websites, earnings releases, and other filings, I landed on a company with a compelling story. It was in the middle of a turnaround, and by the end of my analysis I felt confident it was a good buy.
The problem was the process. I had no way of knowing whether I would get that luck again, or if I would waste two hours scanning tickers and then spend days digging into a single name with nothing to show for it.

**The Solution**
I built something better. I wrote a Python program that scans all 7,000+ microcap stocks listed on the NYSE and NASDAQ. The program evaluates each company on a quantitative basis to determine whether it warrants further analysis. It first applies a set of automatic disqualifiers to clear out the obvious noise, then uses metrics like revenue trends, FCF yield, ROIC, ROE, and P/B ratio (among others) to rank the remaining universe and surface the top 50.

**What You Can Expect**
My goal is straightforward. I will further filter that top 50 and publish the top 10 here on the site as a resource for you. Once my next project is complete, I will begin posting full analyst-level research on the top 5 companies. Both lists will be updated monthly.
Most microcap stocks receive zero coverage from major banks. I am building Allegory to be a resource for retail investors who do their own work and for smaller funds whose position sizing allows them to operate in the microcap space.
That is what I am working on now. I will keep uploading information and stress testing my models. The more work I put in, the more I learn, and the more I can provide for you. I look forward to building this, and I hope it earns your attention.
